query_summary: Large Volume Customer — find customers/orders where total lineitem quantity exceeds 300, return top-100 by o_totalprice DESC, o_orderdate ASC

analysis.subquery_cardinality_estimate: ~500–1000 qualifying orderkeys (SUM(l_quantity)>300 is extremely selective at SF10; ~0.003–0.007% of 15M orders)
analysis.key_insight: The subquery SUM(l_quantity) per l_orderkey IS the outer query's SUM(l_quantity) for qualifying groups — a single lineitem scan computes both. No second lineitem pass needed.
analysis.dominant_phase: lineitem full scan + partitioned aggregation (60M rows, 2 columns)
analysis.join_ordering_rationale: After subquery pre-computation: qualifying_orderkeys (~1K) → probe orders (15M) to get ~1K qualifying order rows → probe customer flat-array for c_name. All probe phases are trivially fast relative to the lineitem scan.

join_order.operation: lineitem full scan → hash aggregate SUM(l_quantity) per l_orderkey
join_order.output: compact_hash_map<int32_t, int64_t> orderkey_to_sumqty (~15M unique keys during build, ~1K survive HAVING filter)
join_order.note: Single pass over lineitem satisfies both the IN-subquery and the outer SUM(l_quantity)

join_order.operation: Filter orderkey_to_sumqty map: keep entries where sum_qty > 300*100 (scaled int64)
join_order.output: qualifying_set: compact_hash_map<int32_t, int64_t> with ~1K entries (orderkey → sum_qty)

join_order.operation: orders full scan → semi-join probe qualifying_set on o_orderkey
join_order.probe: orders (15M rows), columns: o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice
join_order.output: qualifying_orders vector<{o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice, sum_qty}> (~1K rows)

join_order.operation: customer full scan → build flat_array[c_custkey] = c_name_string_offset
join_order.note: c_custkey is dense integer [1, 1500000] — flat array of size 1500001 gives O(1) lookup with zero hash overhead. Load c_custkey + c_name only.

join_order.operation: Enrich qualifying_orders with c_name via flat array lookup (trivial, ~1K probes)
join_order.output: result_rows vector<{c_name, c_custkey, o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice, sum_qty}>

join_order.operation: GROUP BY (c_name, c_custkey, o_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice) → trivially 1 row per (o_orderkey) since o_orderkey is unique per order
join_order.note: No actual grouping work needed — each qualifying order is already 1 row with sum_qty pre-computed

join_order.operation: TopK(100) by o_totalprice DESC, o_orderdate ASC using max-heap of size 100
join_order.note: Result set is ~1K rows; partial_sort is sufficient and fast

logical_plan.subquery_detail: Decorrelate IN(subquery) to single-pass lineitem aggregation. Build compact_hash_map<int32_t,int64_t> of orderkey→sum_qty across all 60M lineitems. Filter map entries where sum_qty > 30000 (300 * scale_factor=100) to produce qualifying_set. Reuse sum_qty for outer SUM(l_quantity).
logical_plan.aggregation: hash_group_by (phase 1: partitioned parallel over lineitem for 15M-group aggregation; phase 2: trivial since qualifying groups ~1K)

filter_pushdown.note: ~1K out of ~15M unique orderkeys survive the HAVING filter
Semi-join probe reduces 15M orders to ~1K rows

late_materialization.customer: Scan c_custkey + c_name only (c_name needed for output but customer scan is cheap at 1.5M rows)
late_materialization.lineitem: Scan l_orderkey + l_quantity only (2 columns out of many)

data_structures.lineitem_agg_phase1: partitioned_hash_map(32 partitions, int32_t→int64_t, pre_sized ~500K slots per partition) for parallel thread-local SUM(l_quantity) per l_orderkey
data_structures.lineitem_agg_merge: After parallel phase, merge 64 thread-local partitioned maps → single compact_hash_map<int32_t,int64_t> (~15M entries total)
data_structures.qualifying_set: compact_hash_map<int32_t,int64_t>(pre_sized ~2048 slots) mapping qualifying orderkey→sum_qty_scaled; built by filtering merged agg map
data_structures.customer_lookup: flat_array<std::string>(1500001) indexed directly by c_custkey — O(1) lookup, zero hash overhead, fits in ~500MB (or store offsets into a string pool)
data_structures.qualifying_orders_buffer: std::vector<{int32_t o_orderkey, int32_t o_custkey, int32_t o_orderdate, int64_t o_totalprice, int64_t sum_qty}> expected ~1K entries
data_structures.topk_heap: std::priority_queue<result_row, vector, comparator>(size=100) for O(n log 100) Top-K extraction

phases.method: OpenMP parallel for over 60M rows in 65K-row morsels
phases.thread_local: Each thread maintains its own partitioned_hash_map(32 partitions) for l_orderkey→sum_qty accumulation
phases.synchronization: thread_local_merge — no locks during scan; sequential merge of 64 thread-local maps after parallel phase

phases.method: OpenMP parallel for over 15M rows; qualifying_set is read-only (no synchronization needed)
phases.output_collection: Thread-local result vectors, concatenated after parallel phase

phases.method: Single-threaded or parallel scan of 1.5M rows to populate flat_array
phases.note: Can be parallelized but 1.5M rows is fast enough single-threaded (~5ms); parallel only if overlapping with other phases

index_usage.rationale: Full lineitem scan is required for the subquery aggregation over all 60M rows. Index lookup would add overhead over a sequential columnar scan. The qualifying_set (post-filter) is used to probe orders instead.
After orders semi-join filter produces ~1K qualifying orders, customer lookup is done via flat_array on c_custkey — more cache-friendly than index indirection.
Replaced by flat_array[c_custkey] → O(1) direct indexed lookup with no hash overhead.

scan_details.lineitem: Fused scan of l_orderkey + l_quantity (2 columns only). Sequential block reads of 65K rows, parallel across threads. No predicate filtering during scan — all rows feed into hash aggregation.
scan_details.orders: Fused scan of o_orderkey + o_custkey + o_orderdate + o_totalprice (4 columns). Parallel morsel scan with inline qualifying_set probe per row.
scan_details.customer: Fused scan of c_custkey + c_name (2 columns). Single pass to build flat_array lookup structure.

memory_details.lineitem_agg: Thread-local partitioned maps avoid false sharing. ~15M unique l_orderkeys × 8 bytes value + 4 bytes key × load_factor = ~360MB across all threads (thread-local ~5.6MB each at 64 threads)
memory_details.qualifying_set: ~1K entries × 12 bytes = negligible (~12KB), fits in L1 cache
memory_details.customer_flat_array: 1.5M entries; string storage via string_pool with 16-byte average name → ~24MB total, fits in L3 cache (44MB)
memory_details.orders_result_buffer: ~1K rows × ~24 bytes = ~24KB, trivially in L1 cache

output_encoding.o_orderdate: Convert from days_since_epoch to YYYY-MM-DD for output

estimated_cost.target_comparison: DuckDB=175ms, Umbra=180ms — plan targets sub-175ms via single lineitem pass + flat-array customer lookup + 64-thread parallelism
